expanded_description: Understanding how lone pairs of electrons influence molecular geometry by exerting greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing bond angles, and occupying specific positions to minimize electron-electron repulsion in VSEPR theory.

core_concept.principle: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs because they are closer to the central atom and occupy more space, causing bond angles to compress from ideal values and determining preferred positions in molecules with steric numbers 5 and 6.
core_concept.explanation: Lone pairs are nonbonding electron pairs localized on the central atom. Unlike bonding pairs which are shared between two nuclei and pulled away from the central atom, lone pairs are held by only one nucleus and spread out more, occupying greater volume. This creates a repulsion hierarchy: lone pair-lone pair (LP-LP) > lone pair-bonding pair (LP-BP) > bonding pair-bonding pair (BP-BP). The stronger repulsion from lone pairs compresses bond angles between bonding pairs and determines which positions lone pairs occupy in trigonal bipyramidal and octahedral geometries.

image.alt: Diagram comparing CH₄, NH₃, and H₂O showing progressive bond angle compression from 109.5° to 107° to 104.5°
image.caption: Lone pairs compress bond angles: CH₄ (0 LP, 109.5°) > NH₃ (1 LP, 107°) > H₂O (2 LP, 104.5°)

sections.section_title: Why Lone Pairs Exert Greater Repulsion
sections.introduction: Understanding why lone pairs exert stronger repulsion than bonding pairs is fundamental to predicting molecular geometries and bond angle deviations. This difference in repulsion strength explains why molecules with the same steric number can have different shapes and bond angles.

key_concept.definition: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs because they are localized on the central atom, occupy more space, and are held by only one nucleus rather than being shared between two nuclei.
key_concept.context: This repulsion difference creates a hierarchy: LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP, which determines molecular geometry variations and bond angle compression.

principles.statement: Lone pairs are localized on the central atom and held by only one nucleus, while bonding pairs are shared between two nuclei and pulled away from the central atom.
principles.application: Explains why lone pairs occupy more space near the central atom and exert stronger repulsion on neighboring electron domains.
principles.example: In NH₃, the lone pair on N is held only by N nucleus and spreads out more than N-H bonding pairs which are pulled toward H atoms
principles.analogy: Like a person sitting alone on a bench (lone pair) takes up more space than two people sharing a bench (bonding pair)—the lone person spreads out more.

principles.statement: Electron pair repulsions follow the order: LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP, with lone pair interactions causing the strongest repulsion.
principles.application: Predicts bond angle compression and lone pair positioning in molecules; explains why molecules with lone pairs have smaller bond angles than ideal values.
principles.example: H₂O has 2 LP with strong LP-LP repulsion compressing H-O-H angle to 104.5°; CH₄ has 0 LP with only BP-BP repulsion maintaining 109.5°
principles.analogy: Like different sized balloons at a party: large balloons (lone pairs) push smaller balloons (bonding pairs) closer together.

principles.statement: Each lone pair on a central atom compresses bond angles between bonding pairs by approximately 2-2.5° from ideal values.
principles.application: Enables prediction of approximate bond angles based on lone pair count; explains systematic angle trends in molecular families.
principles.example: Tetrahedral family: CH₄ (0 LP) 109.5° > NH₃ (1 LP) 107° > H₂O (2 LP) 104.5°; each LP compresses ~2-2.5°
principles.analogy: Like adding large furniture to a room: each large piece (lone pair) reduces the space available for other furniture (bonding pairs).

classifications.value: Lone pairs have distinct properties that cause stronger repulsion than bonding pairs
characteristics: Localized on central atom (not shared)
Held by single nucleus only
Occupy more space near central atom
Electron density concentrated closer to central atom
Not involved in bonding
classifications.behavior: Lone pairs spread out more than bonding pairs, creating stronger repulsive fields that compress bonding pair angles

examples.explanation: N in NH₃ has 1 lone pair held only by N nucleus; spreads out more than N-H bonding pairs; compresses H-N-H angles from 109.5° to 107°
O in H₂O has 2 lone pairs; strong LP-LP repulsion plus LP-BP repulsion compresses H-O-H angle to 104.5°

classifications.value: Bonding pairs have properties that cause weaker repulsion than lone pairs
characteristics: Shared between two nuclei
Pulled away from central atom toward bonding partner
Occupy less space near central atom
Electron density distributed between two atoms
Involved in chemical bonding
classifications.behavior: Bonding pairs are more constrained and occupy less volume near central atom, exerting weaker repulsion than lone pairs

examples.explanation: C-H bonding pair shared between C and H; electron density pulled toward both nuclei; occupies less space near C than lone pair would
Double/triple bonds have higher electron density than single bonds; exert stronger repulsion than single bonds but still less than lone pairs

classifications.value: Quantitative comparison of repulsion strengths between different electron pair types
characteristics: LP-LP repulsion: strongest (~1.0 relative strength)
LP-BP repulsion: intermediate (~0.8 relative strength)
BP-BP repulsion: weakest (~0.6 relative strength)
Multiple bond-BP: stronger than single bond-BP but weaker than LP-BP
classifications.behavior: Repulsion strength determines bond angle compression magnitude and lone pair positioning preferences

examples.explanation: CH₄ (only BP-BP): 109.5°; NH₃ (LP-BP dominant): 107°; H₂O (LP-LP + LP-BP): 104.5°
C=O double bond exerts stronger repulsion than C-H single bonds; H-C-H angle compressed to ~116° from ideal 120°

classifications.value: Several factors influence the magnitude of lone pair repulsion effects
characteristics: Central atom electronegativity: more electronegative atoms hold lone pairs closer, reducing repulsion
Central atom size: larger atoms have lone pairs farther from nucleus, affecting repulsion
Number of lone pairs: more lone pairs = greater cumulative repulsion
Presence of multiple bonds: multiple bonds exert stronger repulsion than single bonds
classifications.behavior: These factors cause variations in bond angle compression beyond the simple 2-2.5° per lone pair rule

examples.explanation: H₂O: 104.5°; H₂S: 92°; S is less electronegative and larger than O; bonding pairs farther from central atom; lone pairs dominate more
NH₃: 107°; PH₃: 93°; P less electronegative than N; similar effect as H₂O vs. H₂S

worked_examples.title: Explaining Bond Angle Compression in NH₃
worked_examples.problem: Explain why the H-N-H bond angle in ammonia (NH₃) is 107° instead of the ideal tetrahedral angle of 109.5°.

step_by_step.explanation: N has 4 electron domains (3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair) → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
NH₃ has 1 lone pair on N atom
Lone pair exerts stronger repulsion than bonding pairs (LP-BP > BP-BP)
step_by_step.calculation: LP pushes bonding pairs closer together

step_by_step.explanation: Lone pair occupies more space near N; bonding pairs compressed to reduce LP-BP repulsion
step_by_step.calculation: H-N-H angle compressed from 109.5° to 107° (~2.5° compression)

step_by_step.explanation: Each lone pair compresses bond angles by approximately 2-2.5° from ideal values

worked_examples.final_answer: NH₃ bond angle is 107° (not 109.5°) because the lone pair on N exerts stronger repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing H-N-H angles by ~2.5°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs, compressing bond angles from ideal values.

worked_examples.title: Comparing H₂O and H₂S Bond Angles
worked_examples.problem: Explain why H₂O has a bond angle of 104.5° while H₂S has a bond angle of only 92°, even though both have 2 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs.

step_by_step.explanation: Both O and S have 4 electron domains (2 BP + 2 LP) → tetrahedral electron domain geometry
O electronegativity = 3.44; S electronegativity = 2.58; O is more electronegative
More electronegative O pulls bonding electrons closer to itself, away from central atom; bonding pairs occupy less space near central atom
step_by_step.calculation: H₂O: bonding pairs closer to O; H₂S: bonding pairs closer to H

step_by_step.explanation: In H₂S, bonding pairs are farther from central atom, so lone pairs exert relatively greater repulsion; bond angles compress more
step_by_step.calculation: H₂S: greater LP dominance → smaller angle

step_by_step.explanation: S is larger than O; bonding pairs are farther from nucleus and from each other; lone pair repulsion dominates more
step_by_step.calculation: Larger central atom → smaller bond angles with same lone pair count

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂S has smaller bond angle (92°) than H₂O (104.5°) because S is less electronegative and larger than O; bonding pairs are farther from central atom in H₂S, allowing lone pairs to compress bond angles more
worked_examples.concept_applied: Central atom electronegativity and size affect bond angles in addition to lone pair count; less electronegative, larger central atoms show greater angle compression.

sections.key_concept: Bond angles systematically decrease as lone pair count increases within a steric number family. Understanding these trends enables prediction of approximate bond angles for molecules based on their lone pair count.

categories.description: Most common example of lone pair bond angle compression; three molecular geometries with progressive angle reduction.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Each lone pair compresses bond angles by ~2-2.5° from ideal 109.5°; LP-LP repulsion strongest, causing greatest compression in H₂O.

examples.explanation: 4 BP, 0 LP; tetrahedral; 109.5° bond angles; no lone pair compression
3 BP, 1 LP; trigonal pyramidal; ~107° bond angles; ~2.5° compression from 1 LP
2 BP, 2 LP; bent; ~104.5° bond angles; ~5° compression from 2 LP

categories.description: Two molecular geometries with lone pair causing moderate angle compression from ideal 120°.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Single lone pair compresses bond angles by ~1-2° from ideal 120°; less compression than tetrahedral family due to more space in planar arrangement.

examples.explanation: 3 BP, 0 LP; trigonal planar; 120° bond angles; no lone pair compression
2 BP, 1 LP; bent; ~119° bond angles; ~1° compression from 1 LP
2 BP, 1 LP; bent; ~117° bond angles; ~3° compression (resonance affects angle)

categories.description: Four molecular geometries with complex angle variations due to axial/equatorial position differences.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lone pairs occupy equatorial positions; compress both axial-equatorial (<90°) and equatorial-equatorial (<120°) angles; compression magnitude varies by position.

examples.explanation: 5 BP, 0 LP; trigonal bipyramidal; 90°/120°/180° angles; no compression
4 BP, 1 LP; seesaw; axial-equatorial <90°, equatorial-equatorial <120°; LP in equatorial position
3 BP, 2 LP; T-shaped; F-Cl-F ~87.5°; significant compression from 2 equatorial LP
2 BP, 3 LP; linear; 180° axial angle unchanged; all 3 LP equatorial

categories.description: Three molecular geometries with lone pair effects depending on symmetry of lone pair placement.
categories.detailed_mechanism: 1 LP compresses all angles below 90°; 2 opposite LP maintain 90° angles due to symmetry; lone pairs occupy trans positions.

examples.explanation: 6 BP, 0 LP; octahedral; all 90° angles; no compression
5 BP, 1 LP; square pyramidal; all angles <90°; LP compresses uniformly
4 BP, 2 LP; square planar; 90° angles maintained; opposite LP don't compress due to symmetry

classifications.value: Approximate bond angle compression per lone pair for different steric numbers
characteristics: SN = 4 (tetrahedral): ~2-2.5° per LP
SN = 3 (trigonal planar): ~1-3° per LP
SN = 5 (trigonal bipyramidal): variable by position (axial vs. equatorial)
SN = 6 (octahedral): ~2-5° per LP if asymmetrical; 0° if symmetrical (opposite LP)
classifications.behavior: Compression magnitude depends on steric number, position, and central atom properties

examples.explanation: BrF₅ (1 LP): <90°; XeF₄ (2 LP opposite): 90° (symmetry maintains ideal angle)

classifications.value: Central atom properties modify lone pair compression beyond simple count
characteristics: Electronegativity: more electronegative → less compression (bonding pairs held closer)
Atomic size: larger atom → more compression (bonding pairs farther away)
Period: period 3+ shows greater compression than period 2 for same lone pair count
Oxidation state: higher oxidation state → less compression (more electron withdrawal)
classifications.behavior: These factors explain why molecules with same lone pair count can have different bond angles

examples.explanation: NH₃ (period 2): 107°; PH₃ (period 3): 93°; larger P allows greater LP compression
H₂O (O EN=3.44): 104.5°; H₂S (S EN=2.58): 92°; less electronegative S shows greater compression

classifications.value: Multiple bonds exert stronger repulsion than single bonds, affecting bond angles
characteristics: Double bond repulsion > single bond repulsion
Triple bond repulsion > double bond repulsion
Multiple bond repulsion < lone pair repulsion
Multiple bonds compress adjacent single bond angles
classifications.behavior: Multiple bonds act like 'intermediate' repulsion between lone pairs and single bonds

examples.explanation: C=O double bond exerts stronger repulsion than C-H single bonds; H-C-H angle compressed to ~116° from ideal 120°
Resonance makes all bonds equivalent; O-N-O angles = 120° (no compression from bond type differences)

worked_examples.title: Predicting Bond Angles in Tetrahedral Family
worked_examples.problem: Predict the approximate bond angles for CH₄, NH₃, and H₂O based on their lone pair counts.

step_by_step.step: Identify steric number for all three
step_by_step.explanation: All have SN = 4 (tetrahedral electron domain geometry)
CH₄: 0 LP; NH₃: 1 LP; H₂O: 2 LP
step_by_step.calculation: CH₄: 0 LP; NH₃: 1 LP; H₂O: 2 LP

step_by_step.explanation: Each LP compresses ~2-2.5° from ideal 109.5°
1 LP × ~2.5° compression
2 LP × ~2.5° compression

worked_examples.final_answer: CH₄: 109.5° (0 LP); NH₃: ~107° (1 LP, ~2.5° compression); H₂O: ~104.5° (2 LP, ~5° compression)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Each lone pair compresses tetrahedral bond angles by approximately 2-2.5° from ideal 109.5°.

worked_examples.title: Explaining XeF₄ Bond Angle Maintenance
worked_examples.problem: Explain why XeF₄ has 90° bond angles despite having 2 lone pairs, while BrF₅ with 1 lone pair has bond angles <90°.

step_by_step.explanation: Both have SN = 6 (octahedral electron domain geometry)
BrF₅: 1 LP; XeF₄: 2 LP
BrF₅: 1 LP in any position (all equivalent in octahedral); XeF₄: 2 LP in opposite (trans) positions
XeF₄'s opposite lone pairs create symmetrical repulsion; bonding pairs compressed equally in all directions
BrF₅'s single lone pair creates asymmetrical repulsion; bonding pairs compressed toward lone pair
Symmetrical lone pair placement can maintain ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement compresses angles
step_by_step.calculation: Symmetry determines whether lone pairs compress angles

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₄ maintains 90° bond angles because 2 opposite lone pairs create symmetrical repulsion; BrF₅ has <90° angles because 1 lone pair creates asymmetrical repulsion that compresses bonding pairs
worked_examples.concept_applied: Symmetrical lone pair placement (opposite positions) can maintain ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement compresses angles.

sections.key_concept: Lone pairs occupy specific positions in trigonal bipyramidal and octahedral geometries to minimize repulsion. Understanding these positioning rules is essential for predicting correct molecular geometries.

process_flow.description: Determine total electron domains (bonding + lone pairs) on central atom.
process_flow.result: Steric number (5 or 6 for advanced geometries)

process_flow.description: Determine number of lone pairs on central atom.
SN = 5: lone pairs go equatorial; SN = 6: lone pairs go opposite (trans).
Based on atom positions only; lone pairs influence shape but not named.

classifications.value: Lone pairs in trigonal bipyramidal geometry always occupy equatorial positions
characteristics: Equatorial positions have 2 × 90° interactions (with axial domains)
Axial positions have 3 × 90° interactions (with equatorial domains)
Lone pairs minimize repulsion by choosing position with fewer 90° interactions
All lone pairs go equatorial (up to 3 lone pairs possible)
classifications.behavior: Equatorial preference explains seesaw (1 LP), T-shaped (2 LP), and linear (3 LP) molecular geometries

examples.explanation: Lone pair occupies equatorial position; 4 F atoms form seesaw shape; minimizes 90° LP-BP interactions
Both lone pairs occupy equatorial positions (120° apart); 3 F atoms form T-shape; minimizes LP-LP repulsion
All 3 lone pairs occupy all 3 equatorial positions (120° apart); 2 F atoms in axial positions form linear shape

classifications.value: Multiple lone pairs in octahedral geometry occupy opposite (trans) positions
characteristics: 1 lone pair: any position (all equivalent in octahedral)
2 lone pairs: opposite positions (180° apart)
Maximizes LP-LP separation
Maintains molecular symmetry when possible
classifications.behavior: Trans positioning explains square pyramidal (1 LP) and square planar (2 LP) molecular geometries

examples.explanation: Single lone pair can occupy any position; 5 F atoms form square pyramid; all positions equivalent in octahedral
2 lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions; 4 F atoms form square plane; maximizes LP-LP separation to 180°
2 lone pairs opposite each other; 4 Cl atoms in square planar arrangement; same as XeF₄

classifications.value: Count 90° interactions to understand why lone pairs prefer specific positions
characteristics: SN = 5 axial position: 3 × 90° interactions
SN = 5 equatorial position: 2 × 90° interactions
SN = 6: all positions have 4 × 90° interactions (equivalent)
Lone pairs choose positions with fewer 90° interactions
classifications.behavior: 90° interactions cause strongest repulsion; minimizing them minimizes total repulsion

examples.explanation: Equatorial LP: 2 × 90° LP-BP interactions; Axial LP would have: 3 × 90° LP-BP interactions; equatorial preferred
2 LP adjacent: 90° LP-LP interaction (strong); 2 LP opposite: 180° LP-LP separation (minimized); opposite preferred

classifications.type: Molecular Geometry from Lone Pair Positions
classifications.value: Lone pair positions determine molecular geometry names and shapes
characteristics: SN = 5: 0 LP = trigonal bipyramidal, 1 LP = seesaw, 2 LP = T-shaped, 3 LP = linear
SN = 6: 0 LP = octahedral, 1 LP = square pyramidal, 2 LP = square planar
Molecular geometry based on atom positions only
Lone pairs influence shape but not included in geometry name
classifications.behavior: Systematic relationship between lone pair count/position and molecular geometry

examples.explanation: PCl₅ (0 LP) → SF₄ (1 LP) → ClF₃ (2 LP) → XeF₂ (3 LP); progressive replacement of atoms with lone pairs
SF₆ (0 LP) → BrF₅ (1 LP) → XeF₄ (2 LP); progressive replacement with opposite LP placement

worked_examples.title: Determining Lone Pair Position in SF₄
worked_examples.problem: Explain why the lone pair in SF₄ occupies an equatorial position rather than an axial position in the trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry.

step_by_step.explanation: 5 electron domains: 3 equatorial (120° apart) + 2 axial (90° to equatorial)
Axial position has 3 × 90° interactions with equatorial domains
Equatorial position has 2 × 90° interactions with axial domains
Equatorial position has fewer 90° interactions (2 vs. 3); less repulsion
Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize 90° interactions to reduce repulsion

worked_examples.final_answer: Lone pair in SF₄ occupies equatorial position because it has only 2 × 90° interactions vs. 3 × 90° for axial position; minimizes LP-BP repulsion
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize 90° interactions; equatorial positions in trigonal bipyramidal have fewer 90° interactions than axial.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for ICl₄⁻
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for the iodine tetrachloride ion (ICl₄⁻) and explain the lone pair positions.

step_by_step.explanation: I is central atom with 7 valence electrons; -1 charge means 1 extra electron (8 total); forms 4 single bonds with Cl atoms; I has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: I: 7 valence e⁻ + 1 (charge) = 8 e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 6 electron domains
6 electron domains → octahedral electron domain geometry
2 lone pairs occupy opposite (trans) positions (180° apart) to minimize LP-LP repulsion
4 atoms in plane with 2 lone pairs above and below → square planar
Square planar has 90° bond angles; opposite lone pairs don't compress angles due to symmetry

worked_examples.final_answer: ICl₄⁻: Steric number = 6; Electron domain geometry = octahedral; Molecular geometry = square planar; Lone pairs opposite (trans); Bond angles = 90°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Steric number 6 with 2 lone pairs gives square planar molecular geometry; lone pairs occupy opposite positions to minimize repulsion.

sections.section_title: Comparing Lone Pair Effects Across Geometry Families
sections.key_concept: Lone pair effects vary systematically across different steric number families. Understanding these patterns enables prediction of molecular geometries and bond angles for any molecule.

components.definition: Lone pair effects include bond angle compression, position preferences, and molecular geometry variations that follow predictable patterns based on steric number.
components.equation: Repulsion hierarchy: LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP; Bond angle ≈ Ideal angle - (number of LP × compression per LP)
components.interpretation: Systematic lone pair effects enable prediction of molecular geometries without memorizing every molecule; understand patterns rather than individual cases.

image.alt: Comparison chart showing lone pair effects across SN = 3, 4, 5, and 6 geometries
image.caption: Lone pair effects vary by steric number: SN=3 (120°→<120°), SN=4 (109.5°→107°→104.5°), SN=5 (equatorial preference), SN=6 (trans preference)

principles.statement: The magnitude and nature of lone pair effects depend on steric number; different geometry families show different compression patterns.
principles.application: Enables prediction of bond angle trends across different steric numbers; explains why SN = 6 with 2 LP can maintain ideal angles while SN = 4 with 2 LP cannot.
principles.example: SN = 4 with 2 LP (H₂O): 104.5° (5° compression); SN = 6 with 2 LP (XeF₄): 90° (0° compression due to symmetry)
principles.analogy: Like different sized rooms: adding furniture (lone pairs) affects small rooms (SN = 4) more than large rooms (SN = 6).

principles.statement: Symmetrical lone pair placement can maintain ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement causes angle compression.
principles.application: Explains why XeF₄ (2 opposite LP) has 90° angles while BrF₅ (1 LP) has <90° angles; symmetry determines compression.
principles.example: XeF₄: 2 LP opposite → symmetrical → 90° maintained; BrF₅: 1 LP → asymmetrical → <90°
principles.analogy: Like balancing weights on a scale: equal weights on opposite sides (symmetrical) maintain balance; single weight (asymmetrical) tips the scale.

principles.statement: Lone pairs occupy positions that minimize 90° repulsive interactions, which vary by geometry family.
principles.application: Predicts lone pair positions: SN = 5 → equatorial (2 × 90° vs. 3 × 90° for axial); SN = 6 → opposite (180° LP-LP separation).
principles.example: SF₄: LP equatorial (2 × 90°); ClF₃: 2 LP equatorial (120° LP-LP); XeF₄: 2 LP opposite (180° LP-LP)
principles.analogy: Like choosing seating at a party: choose seats with fewer close neighbors to minimize uncomfortable interactions.

classifications.type: Bond Angle Compression by Steric Number
classifications.value: Systematic comparison of lone pair compression across different steric numbers
characteristics: SN = 5: variable by position (equatorial LP compresses both 90° and 120° angles)
SN = 6: ~2-5° per LP if asymmetrical; 0° if symmetrical (opposite LP)
classifications.behavior: Compression magnitude depends on available space and geometry symmetry

examples.explanation: SN = 3 (SO₂): 120° → ~119° (~1° per LP); SN = 4 (H₂O): 109.5° → 104.5° (~2.5° per LP); more compression in tetrahedral
BrF₅ (1 LP, asymmetrical): <90°; XeF₄ (2 LP opposite, symmetrical): 90°; symmetry can eliminate compression

classifications.value: Different geometry families use different strategies to minimize lone pair repulsion
characteristics: SN = 3: no position choice (all equivalent in trigonal planar)
SN = 4: no position choice (all equivalent in tetrahedral)
SN = 5: equatorial preference (fewer 90° interactions)
SN = 6: trans preference (maximize LP-LP separation)
classifications.behavior: Position strategies reflect geometry symmetry and available positions

examples.explanation: All lone pairs go equatorial: SF₄ (1 LP eq), ClF₃ (2 LP eq), XeF₂ (3 LP eq)
Multiple LP go opposite: BrF₅ (1 LP any), XeF₄ (2 LP trans)

classifications.value: Systematic progression of molecular geometries as lone pairs replace bonding pairs
characteristics: SN = 4: tetrahedral (0 LP) → trigonal pyramidal (1 LP) → bent (2 LP)
SN = 5: trigonal bipyramidal (0 LP) → seesaw (1 LP) → T-shaped (2 LP) → linear (3 LP)
SN = 6: octahedral (0 LP) → square pyramidal (1 LP) → square planar (2 LP)
Each LP replaces one bonding pair systematically
classifications.behavior: Progressive replacement creates predictable geometry sequence

examples.explanation: CH₄ → NH₃ → H₂O; tetrahedral → trigonal pyramidal → bent
PCl₅ → SF₄ → ClF₃ → XeF₂; trigonal bipyramidal → seesaw → T-shaped → linear
SF₆ → BrF₅ → XeF₄; octahedral → square pyramidal → square planar

classifications.value: Central atom properties modify lone pair effects beyond simple steric number predictions
characteristics: Electronegativity: more EN → less compression (bonding pairs held closer to central atom)
Atomic size: larger atom → more compression (bonding pairs farther from central atom)
Period: period 3+ shows greater compression than period 2
These effects can override simple lone pair count predictions
classifications.behavior: Central atom properties explain deviations from ideal compression patterns

examples.explanation: NH₃ (period 2): 107°; PH₃ (period 3): 93°; larger P allows greater LP compression despite same lone pair count
H₂O (O EN=3.44): 104.5°; H₂S (S EN=2.58): 92°; less EN allows greater compression

worked_examples.title: Comparing Lone Pair Effects in SN = 4 vs. SN = 6
worked_examples.problem: Explain why H₂O (SN = 4, 2 LP) has bond angles of 104.5° while XeF₄ (SN = 6, 2 LP) has bond angles of 90° (ideal), despite both having 2 lone pairs.

step_by_step.explanation: Both have 2 lone pairs on central atom
H₂O: SN = 4 (tetrahedral); XeF₄: SN = 6 (octahedral)
H₂O: 2 LP in tetrahedral arrangement (~109.5° apart); XeF₄: 2 LP in opposite positions (180° apart)
step_by_step.calculation: H₂O: LP ~109.5° apart; XeF₄: LP 180° apart

step_by_step.explanation: H₂O: asymmetrical LP placement compresses bonding pairs; XeF₄: symmetrical opposite LP placement maintains bonding pair symmetry
H₂O's asymmetrical LP compress H-O-H angle to 104.5°; XeF₄'s symmetrical LP don't compress F-Xe-F angles (remain 90°)
Symmetrical lone pair placement can maintain ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement causes compression
step_by_step.calculation: Symmetry > lone pair count for predicting compression

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂O has 104.5° angles (compressed from 109.5°) because 2 LP are asymmetrical in tetrahedral arrangement; XeF₄ has 90° angles (ideal) because 2 LP are symmetrical (opposite) in octahedral arrangement; symmetry determines compression, not just lone pair count
worked_examples.concept_applied: Symmetrical lone pair placement can maintain ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement causes compression regardless of lone pair count.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Geometry for Unknown Molecule
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and approximate bond angles for a molecule with steric number 5 and 2 lone pairs.

step_by_step.explanation: SN = 5 → trigonal bipyramidal electron domain geometry
SN = 5: lone pairs occupy equatorial positions
5 domains - 2 LP = 3 bonding pairs; 3 atoms with 2 equatorial LP → T-shaped
Ideal trigonal bipyramidal: 90° (axial-equatorial), 120° (equatorial-equatorial); 2 equatorial LP compress angles
ClF₃ has SN = 5, 2 LP; T-shaped geometry; F-Cl-F angle ~87.5°

worked_examples.final_answer: SN = 5, 2 LP: Electron domain = trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry = T-shaped; Bond angles <90° (~87.5°); Lone pairs occupy equatorial positions
worked_examples.concept_applied: SN = 5 with 2 lone pairs gives T-shaped molecular geometry; equatorial lone pairs compress axial-equatorial angles below 90°.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about lone pair effects can lead to incorrect geometry and bond angle predictions. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of VSEPR theory.

classifications.type: Error: Assuming All Lone Pairs Compress Equally
classifications.value: Thinking all lone pairs compress bond angles by the same amount regardless of geometry
characteristics: Predicting H₂O and XeF₄ both have compressed angles
Not considering symmetry effects
Overlooking steric number differences
classifications.behavior: Remember: symmetrical lone pair placement (like XeF₄ opposite LP) can maintain ideal angles; asymmetrical placement causes compression

examples.explanation: Wrong: <90° due to 2 LP; Correct: 90° (symmetrical opposite LP maintain ideal angles)

classifications.type: Error: Incorrect Lone Pair Positions for SN = 5
classifications.value: Placing lone pairs in axial positions for trigonal bipyramidal geometry
characteristics: Predicting wrong molecular geometry
Not minimizing 90° interactions
Incorrect bond angle predictions
classifications.behavior: Remember: SN = 5 lone pairs always go equatorial (2 × 90° interactions) not axial (3 × 90° interactions)

examples.explanation: Wrong: LP axial; Correct: LP equatorial → seesaw geometry
Wrong: 1 LP axial, 1 LP equatorial; Correct: both LP equatorial → T-shaped geometry

classifications.type: Error: Incorrect Lone Pair Positions for SN = 6
classifications.value: Placing 2 lone pairs adjacent instead of opposite for octahedral geometry
characteristics: Predicting wrong molecular geometry
Not maximizing LP-LP separation
Incorrect bond angle predictions
classifications.behavior: Remember: SN = 6 with 2 lone pairs → lone pairs opposite (trans, 180° apart) → square planar

examples.explanation: Wrong: LP adjacent (90° apart); Correct: LP opposite (180° apart) → square planar geometry

classifications.type: Error: Confusing Electron Domain and Molecular Geometry
classifications.value: Using electron domain geometry name when molecular geometry is different due to lone pairs
characteristics: Calling NH₃ tetrahedral instead of trigonal pyramidal
Calling H₂O tetrahedral instead of bent
Not distinguishing between electron pair arrangement and atom arrangement
classifications.behavior: Remember: electron domain geometry includes lone pairs; molecular geometry names only atom positions

examples.explanation: Wrong: tetrahedral; Correct: electron domain = tetrahedral, molecular geometry = trigonal pyramidal
Wrong: tetrahedral; Correct: electron domain = tetrahedral, molecular geometry = bent

classifications.value: Predicting excessive bond angle compression beyond typical 2-2.5° per lone pair
characteristics: Predicting H₂O has 100° angle
Not recognizing compression limits
Confusing central atom effects with lone pair effects
classifications.behavior: Remember: typical compression is ~2-2.5° per LP for SN = 4; central atom effects can modify but not drastically change this

examples.explanation: Wrong: 100°; Correct: 104.5° (~5° total compression from 2 LP)
H₂S: 92° (not typical); this is due to central atom effects (S less EN, larger) not just lone pair count

classifications.value: Assuming molecules with same lone pair count have identical bond angles
characteristics: Predicting NH₃ and PH₃ have same angle
Not considering electronegativity differences
Not considering atomic size differences
classifications.behavior: Remember: central atom EN and size modify lone pair effects; period 3+ shows greater compression than period 2

examples.explanation: Wrong: both 107°; Correct: NH₃ 107°, PH₃ 93° (P less EN, larger → greater compression)
Wrong: both 104.5°; Correct: H₂O 104.5°, H₂S 92° (S less EN, larger → greater compression)

sections.section_title: Practice Problems: Lone Pair Effects on Molecular Shape
sections.introduction: Test your understanding of lone pair effects with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces understanding of lone pair repulsion, bond angle compression, positioning rules, and geometry predictions.
key_concept.context: Focus on systematic approach: count lone pairs → apply compression rules → determine positions → predict geometry and angles.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Rank the following molecules in order of decreasing bond angle: CH₄, NH₃, H₂O. Explain your ranking.

step_by_step.explanation: All have SN = 4 (tetrahedral electron domain geometry)
CH₄: 0 LP; NH₃: 1 LP; H₂O: 2 LP
step_by_step.calculation: CH₄: 0 LP; NH₃: 1 LP; H₂O: 2 LP

step_by_step.explanation: More lone pairs = greater compression from ideal 109.5°
Fewer lone pairs = larger bond angle

worked_examples.final_answer: Decreasing bond angle: CH₄ (109.5°) > NH₃ (107°) > H₂O (104.5°); more lone pairs cause greater bond angle compression
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lone pairs compress bond angles; more lone pairs = smaller bond angles for same steric number.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry and bond angles for SF₄.

step_by_step.explanation: S is central atom with 6 valence electrons; forms 4 single bonds with F atoms; S has 1 lone pair remaining
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence e⁻; 4 bonds use 4 e⁻; 2 e⁻ remain as 1 LP

step_by_step.explanation: 4 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair = 5 electron domains
5 electron domains → trigonal bipyramidal
SN = 5: lone pair occupies equatorial position
4 atoms + 1 equatorial LP → seesaw
LP compresses angles: axial-equatorial <90°, equatorial-equatorial <120°

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₄: Steric number = 5; Electron domain geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry = seesaw; Lone pair equatorial; Bond angles <90° and <120°
worked_examples.concept_applied: SN = 5 with 1 lone pair gives seesaw molecular geometry; lone pair occupies equatorial position.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Explain why PH₃ has a smaller bond angle (93°) than NH₃ (107°) despite both having the same number of lone pairs.

step_by_step.explanation: Both N and P have 1 lone pair; both SN = 4
N electronegativity = 3.04; P electronegativity = 2.19; N is more electronegative
More electronegative N pulls bonding electrons closer to itself; bonding pairs occupy less space near central atom
step_by_step.calculation: NH₃: bonding pairs closer to N; PH₃: bonding pairs closer to H

step_by_step.explanation: In PH₃, bonding pairs are farther from central atom, so lone pair exerts relatively greater repulsion; bond angles compress more
step_by_step.calculation: PH₃: greater LP dominance → smaller angle

step_by_step.explanation: P is larger than N (period 3 vs. period 2); bonding pairs farther from nucleus; lone pair repulsion dominates more

worked_examples.final_answer: PH₃ has smaller bond angle (93°) than NH₃ (107°) because P is less electronegative and larger than N; bonding pairs are farther from central atom in PH₃, allowing lone pair to compress bond angles more
worked_examples.concept_applied: Central atom electronegativity and size affect bond angles in addition to lone pair count; less electronegative, larger central atoms show greater angle compression.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Predict the molecular geometry for ClF₃ and explain the lone pair positions.

step_by_step.explanation: Cl is central atom with 7 valence electrons; forms 3 single bonds with F atoms; Cl has 2 lone pairs remaining
step_by_step.calculation: Cl: 7 valence e⁻; 3 bonds use 3 e⁻; 4 e⁻ remain as 2 LP

step_by_step.explanation: 3 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 5 electron domains
5 electron domains → trigonal bipyramidal
SN = 5: both lone pairs occupy equatorial positions (120° apart) to minimize LP-LP repulsion
3 atoms + 2 equatorial LP → T-shaped
2 equatorial LP compress axial-equatorial angles below 90°

worked_examples.final_answer: ClF₃: Steric number = 5; Electron domain geometry = trigonal bipyramidal; Molecular geometry = T-shaped; Both lone pairs equatorial; Bond angle <90° (~87.5°)
worked_examples.concept_applied: SN = 5 with 2 lone pairs gives T-shaped molecular geometry; both lone pairs occupy equatorial positions.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Which has bond angles closer to ideal: BrF₅ or XeF₄? Both have steric number 6. Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Both have SN = 6 (octahedral electron domain geometry)
BrF₅: 1 LP; XeF₄: 2 LP
BrF₅: 1 LP in any position (asymmetrical); XeF₄: 2 LP opposite (symmetrical)
BrF₅: <90° (LP compresses angles); XeF₄: 90° (opposite LP maintain symmetry)
XeF₄ has angles closer to ideal (exactly 90°) despite having more lone pairs; symmetry maintains ideal angles

worked_examples.final_answer: XeF₄ has bond angles closer to ideal (exactly 90°) than BrF₅ (<90°) because XeF₄'s 2 opposite lone pairs create symmetrical repulsion that maintains ideal angles, while BrF₅'s 1 lone pair creates asymmetrical repulsion that compresses angles
worked_examples.concept_applied: Symmetrical lone pair placement can maintain ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement compresses angles regardless of lone pair count.

summary: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs because they are localized on the central atom and occupy more space, creating repulsion hierarchy: LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP. This causes systematic bond angle compression: each lone pair compresses tetrahedral angles by ~2-2.5° (CH₄ 109.5° > NH₃ 107° > H₂O 104.5°). Lone pairs occupy specific positions to minimize repulsion: SN = 5 (trigonal bipyramidal) → equatorial positions (2 × 90° interactions vs. 3 × 90° for axial); SN = 6 (octahedral) → opposite/trans positions (180° LP-LP separation). Symmetrical lone pair placement (like XeF₄ opposite LP) can maintain ideal bond angles; asymmetrical placement causes compression. Central atom properties modify lone pair effects: less electronegative and larger atoms show greater compression (NH₃ 107° vs. PH₃ 93°; H₂O 104.5° vs. H₂S 92°). Common errors include: assuming all lone pairs compress equally regardless of symmetry, placing SN = 5 lone pairs in axial positions, placing SN = 6 lone pairs adjacent instead of opposite, confusing electron domain and molecular geometry, overestimating compression, and ignoring central atom effects. Systematic approach: count lone pairs → apply compression rules (~2-2.5° per LP for SN = 4) → determine positions (SN = 5 equatorial, SN = 6 opposite) → predict geometry and angles → consider central atom modifications. Mastery of lone pair effects enables accurate prediction of molecular geometries and bond angles for all VSEPR geometry families.
